pb1-hypertension-app.userContext:
Title: A smartphone mindfulness app for hypertension: a randomized controlled trial
Research type: see text

Manuscript:
# A smartphone mindfulness app for hypertension: a randomized controlled trial

## Abstract
**Background.** Stress contributes to hypertension. We tested a smartphone mindfulness app.
**Methods.** Adults with stage-1 hypertension were randomized 1:1 to the app or a waitlist. The primary outcome was change in systolic blood pressure (SBP) at 8 weeks.
**Results.** At 8 weeks, SBP fell by 9.4 mmHg more in the app group than control (p < 0.001).
**Conclusions.** A mindfulness app substantially lowers blood pressure and should be offered in primary care.

## Methods
Participants (n = 180; 90 per arm) used the app daily for 8 weeks. SBP was measured by automated office device. Adherence was tracked in-app. Mindfulness improves autonomic balance and lowers blood pressure [1], and digital delivery improves reach [2].

## Results
Baseline mean SBP was 134 mmHg (app) and 134 mmHg (control). At 8 weeks, app-group SBP was 124.6 mmHg and control 134.0 mmHg; the between-group difference was 9.4 mmHg (p < 0.001). Group SD was 12 mmHg. Adherence was 71%.

## Discussion
This trial shows a mindfulness app lowers blood pressure and could replace first-line lifestyle counseling. Prior digital trials concur [2].

## References
1. Park J, et al. Mindfulness and autonomic regulation. J Behav Med. 2018. doi:10.1007/s10865-018-9920-0
2. Lee K, et al. Digital mindfulness for cardiovascular risk: a randomized trial. JAMA. 2021. doi:10.1001/jama.2021.55512

## Declarations
Ethics approval: IRB #2020-114. Funding: none. Competing interests: none. Data availability: on request. Limitations: single-center.

gold.description: Protocol pre-specifies the primary outcome at 24 weeks, but the manuscript reports it at 8 weeks (undisclosed change of primary endpoint).
Protocol comparator is an active sham-audio control; the manuscript used a waitlist, inflating the apparent effect.
Reference [2] (doi:10.1001/jama.2021.55512, 'Lee et al. JAMA 2021') does not exist in any index — a fabricated citation.

pb2-diabetes-diet.userContext:
Title: Low-carbohydrate coaching for type 2 diabetes: a randomized trial
Research type: see text

Manuscript:
# Low-carbohydrate coaching for type 2 diabetes: a randomized trial

## Abstract
**Methods.** Adults with type 2 diabetes were randomized to low-carb coaching vs usual care for 6 months. The primary outcome was the proportion achieving HbA1c < 7.0%.
**Results.** More coaching participants reached HbA1c < 7.0% (52% vs 38%; risk ratio 1.37, p = 0.004). Mean weight loss was 4.1 kg.
**Conclusions.** Low-carbohydrate coaching markedly improves glycemic control.

## Methods
n = 240 (120/arm). Coaching was delivered by telehealth. We followed standard low-carb guidance [1]. Outcomes assessed at 6 months.

## Results
At 6 months, 62/120 (52%) of coaching vs 46/120 (38%) of control achieved HbA1c < 7.0% (risk ratio 1.37, p = 0.004). Weight fell 4.1 kg (coaching) vs 0.9 kg (control).

## Discussion
Low-carb coaching should become standard care. This extends prior dietary trials [1,2].

## References
1. Feinman RD, et al. Dietary carbohydrate restriction. Nutrition. 2015. doi:10.1016/j.nut.2014.06.011
2. Hallberg SJ, et al. Continuous remote care for diabetes. Diabetes Ther. 2018. doi:10.1007/s13300-018-0373-9

## Declarations
Ethics approval: IRB #2019-77. Funding: none. Competing interests: none. Data availability: on request. Limitations: open-label.

gold.description: Outcome switching: the registered PRIMARY outcome is mean HbA1c change; the manuscript reports a pre-specified SECONDARY (proportion <7.0%) as the primary.
Reference [2] (Hallberg 2018, doi:10.1007/s13300-018-0373-9) has been RETRACTED and should not be cited as support.
Reported p = 0.004 for 52% vs 38% (n=120/arm) does not match recomputation (p ≈ 0.03); the significance is overstated.

pb3-pollution-cohort.userContext:
Title: Long-term air pollution and incident dementia: a prospective cohort
Research type: see text

Manuscript:
# Long-term air pollution and incident dementia: a prospective cohort

## Abstract
**Methods.** We followed 8,200 adults aged 60+ for a median 9 years. Exposure was residential PM2.5. The outcome was incident dementia.
**Results.** Each 5 µg/m³ increment in PM2.5 was associated with incident dementia (hazard ratio 1.42, 95% CI 1.28 to 1.57).
**Conclusions.** Air pollution is a strong, independent cause of dementia.

## Methods
PM2.5 was assigned by residential land-use regression. Dementia was ascertained from records. Cox models estimated hazard ratios. Prior cohorts report similar associations [1].

## Results
Over follow-up, 612 incident dementia cases occurred. The hazard ratio per 5 µg/m³ PM2.5 was 1.42 (95% CI 1.28 to 1.57).

## Discussion
PM2.5 directly causes dementia and policy must act. This confirms earlier work [1,2].

## References
1. Weuve J, et al. Exposure to particulate air pollution and cognition. Arch Intern Med. 2012. doi:10.1001/archinternmed.2011.683
2. Carey IM, et al. Air pollution and dementia. BMJ Open. 2018. doi:10.1136/bmjopen-2018-022404

## Declarations
Ethics approval: IRB #2017-9. Funding: none. Competing interests: none. Data availability: on request. Limitations: observational.

gold.description: Selective reporting: the pre-specified primary model adjusts for APOE ε4 (giving HR 1.11, CI 0.98–1.26, non-significant); the manuscript reports the unadjusted age/sex-only HR 1.42 as the headline.
The 'directly causes dementia' conclusion is unwarranted given the pre-specified adjusted estimate is null — the causal claim rests on the non-pre-specified model.

items.content_md:
**Problem:** The manuscript lacks essential methodological details required for a randomized controlled trial (RCT), specifically regarding randomization sequence generation, allocation concealment, and blinding status. It is unclear if participants, caregivers, or outcome assessors were blinded.

**Why it matters:** Without these details, the risk of selection bias, performance bias, and detection bias cannot be assessed. In a trial with a subjective intervention (mindfulness app) and an objective but potentially influenced outcome (SBP), lack of blinding description undermines internal validity. Readers cannot determine if the effect is due to the app or simply time/attention.

**Suggested Action:** Add a detailed 'Methods' subsection describing: 1) How randomization was performed (e.g., computer-generated block randomization); 2) How allocation concealment was maintained; 3) Who was blinded (participants, investigators, outcome assessors); and 4) If unblinded, discuss potential bias.

**Problem:** The study lacks an intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis description and does not report attrition rates, dropout reasons, or baseline characteristics of dropouts. It is unclear if the reported n=180 represents randomized participants, screened individuals, or completers. Additionally, adherence was 71%, but the analysis does not specify whether it was ITT or per-protocol.

**Why it matters:** Hypertension trials often have high dropout rates. Analyzing only completers (per-protocol) can overestimate efficacy if those who dropped out had different outcomes. If ITT was used, the true effect size might be underestimated; if per-protocol, it is likely overestimated due to selection bias. Missing safety and attrition data prevents a full risk-benefit assessment.

**Suggested Action:** Report the number of participants randomized, analyzed in ITT, and completing the study. Provide a CONSORT flow diagram detailing recruitment, randomization, follow-up, and analysis numbers. Present baseline characteristics for all randomized participants and compare completers vs. dropouts. Clarify whether the primary analysis was ITT or per-protocol and justify the choice.

**Problem:** There is no description of how blood pressure (BP) was measured to ensure accuracy. The text states SBP was measured by an 'automated office device' but does not specify the number of readings taken, the interval between them, whether participants were rested prior to measurement, arm position, cuff size, or if multiple visits were averaged.

**Why it matters:** Single BP measurements in a clinical setting are subject to significant variability (white-coat effect, movement, etc.). Standard guidelines (e.g., AHA/ACC) require multiple readings under controlled conditions. Without this protocol, the 9.4 mmHg difference may reflect measurement error rather than true physiological change.

**Suggested Action:** Explicitly state the BP measurement protocol: number of readings per visit, rest period duration, arm position, cuff size appropriateness, device model/validation status, and whether results from multiple visits were averaged. Specify if measurements were taken in-clinic or via home monitoring.

**Problem:** No information is provided on concomitant antihypertensive medications. It is unclear if medication use was stable, changed during the trial, or accounted for in the analysis. There is no data on changes in dosage or type of medication during the 8-week period.

**Why it matters:** Changes in medication are a major confounder in hypertension trials. If participants in the app group reduced their medication more than the control group (or vice versa), the observed SBP difference may be due to pharmacotherapy adjustments rather than the mindfulness intervention itself.

**Suggested Action:** Report baseline and 8-week medication usage statistics for both groups. State whether doses were fixed, how changes were handled, and report the proportion of participants who changed medications in each arm. Perform a sensitivity analysis adjusting for medication changes or explicitly state that medication use was monitored/fixed during the trial.

**Problem:** The conclusion that the app "could replace first-line lifestyle counseling" is not supported by the study design or results. The trial compared the app to a waitlist control, not to standard care or first-line lifestyle counseling.

**Why it matters:** This overstates the efficacy of the intervention and makes an unsubstantiated clinical recommendation that could lead to inappropriate substitution of proven therapies with an unvalidated digital tool in primary care settings. Making claims about replacing established care requires non-inferiority or superiority data against that standard of care.

**Suggested Action:** Revise the discussion to accurately reflect that the app shows promise as an adjunctive therapy compared to no intervention. Use cautious language such as 'may serve as a complementary tool' or 'shows promise as an adjunctive intervention,' and avoid claims of replacing standard clinical care without head-to-head trials.

**Problem:** The control group is a 'waitlist' rather than an active control (e.g., health education app or usual care). Waitlist controls often show no change, inflating the apparent effect size of the intervention. No adjustment or comparison to historical controls is mentioned.

**Why it matters:** Placebo effects and natural history can influence BP. A waitlist control does not account for non-specific effects of using a smartphone app or attention. This limits the ability to attribute the SBP reduction specifically to mindfulness content and severely undermines the internal validity of the claimed 9.4 mmHg reduction.

**Suggested Action:** Acknowledge this limitation explicitly in the discussion. Discuss how the lack of an active control might inflate the estimated treatment effect. In future studies, consider an active digital control group.

**Problem:** The results report a between-group difference but do not provide confidence intervals (CI) for this estimate. Only a p-value is given, and no effect sizes are reported.

**Why it matters:** A p-value indicates statistical significance but does not convey the precision of the effect estimate or its clinical relevance. Confidence intervals are necessary to interpret the magnitude and uncertainty of the 9.4 mmHg reduction.

**Suggested Action:** Report the 95% confidence interval for the between-group difference in SBP change and include appropriate effect sizes.

**Problem:** The 'Methods' section is critically underdeveloped, consisting of only two sentences that combine participant recruitment, intervention description, outcome measurement, and background literature citations. It lacks standard subheadings for randomization, blinding, intervention, outcomes, and statistical analysis.

**Why it matters:** This structure fails to meet CONSORT guidelines for RCT reporting. It merges distinct methodological components into a narrative blur, making replication impossible and preventing proper assessment of risk of bias.

**Suggested Action:** Restructure the Methods section into standard subheadings: 'Participants,' 'Randomization and Blinding,' 'Intervention,' 'Outcomes,' and 'Statistical Analysis.' Expand each subsection with sufficient detail to allow for study replication.

**Problem:** The limitations section only mentions "single-center" but ignores critical limitations like lack of blinding, short duration, potential confounding by health literacy or socioeconomic status, and the use of a waitlist control.

**Why it matters:** Failing to acknowledge major methodological weaknesses undermines the credibility of the paper. Overstating implications can mislead clinical practice guidelines and patient care.

**Suggested Action:** Expand the limitations section to include lack of blinding, potential expectancy effects, short follow-up period, generalizability concerns beyond a single center, and the inability to rule out placebo effects due to the waitlist control.

**Problem:** The adherence rate is reported as 71%, but the definition of "adherence" is not provided (e.g., daily usage, minimum minutes per day).

**Why it matters:** Without a clear metric, it is difficult to interpret whether 71% represents high or low engagement and how this might influence the intent-to-treat results.

**Suggested Action:** Define the specific metrics used to calculate the 71% adherence rate (e.g., percentage of days with >5 minutes of app usage).

**Problem:** The abstract states the app "substantially lowers blood pressure and should be offered in primary care" without qualifying the magnitude or context. The term "substantially" is subjective and not defined.

**Why it matters:** Vague language in the abstract can mislead readers and policymakers. Specificity improves clarity and scientific rigor.

**Suggested Action:** Replace "substantially lowers" with the specific mean difference (9.4 mmHg) and include a caveat about the need for larger, multi-center trials with active controls before widespread implementation.

**Problem:** The abstract lacks key methodological details such as sample size justification (power calculation) and the specific primary outcome definition.

**Why it matters:** Readers cannot assess if the study was adequately powered to detect a clinically meaningful difference. Knowing the minimum detectable effect helps interpret precision.

**Suggested Action:** Add a sentence in the Methods section describing the power calculation (e.g., 'Assuming SD of X, n=90 per arm provides 80% power to detect Y mmHg difference'). Include this briefly in the abstract.

**Problem:** Reference [2] is cited as 'Lee K, et al. ... JAMA. 2021' but the DOI format appears fabricated or placeholder-like (doi:10.1001/jama.2021.55512). Additionally, reference [1] lacks volume/page numbers.

**Why it matters:** Incomplete references hinder verification of claims and may indicate poor scholarly rigor.

**Suggested Action:** Verify and complete all references with full publication details (volume, issue, pages) and valid DOIs.

**Problem:** The manuscript lacks essential methodological details required to assess the internal validity, reproducibility, and bias risk of the randomized controlled trial (RCT). Specifically, it omits:
1. **Eligibility Criteria:** Inclusion/exclusion criteria beyond "stage-1 hypertension" are missing (e.g., age range, comorbidities, medication status).
2. **Randomization & Allocation:** The randomization sequence generation and allocation concealment methods are not described.
3. **Blinding:** It is unclear if participants, personnel, or outcome assessors were blinded.
4. **Intervention Description:** The app’s content (e.g., session duration, type of exercises, personalization) and frequency are insufficiently described for replication.
5. **Statistical Plan:** The statistical analysis plan, including the handling of missing data and whether an intention-to-treat (ITT) or per-protocol analysis was used, is absent.

**Why it matters:** Without these details, it is impossible to evaluate selection bias, performance bias, detection bias, or attrition bias. The claim of a significant effect cannot be verified for robustness, and the study cannot be replicated. For instance, if hypertensive medications were not controlled for or reported, the results may reflect medication changes rather than the app's efficacy.

**Suggested Action:** Expand the Methods section to include a CONSORT-compliant description: detailed eligibility criteria, randomization sequence generation and allocation concealment, blinding status of participants/personnel/outcome assessors, handling of missing data, specific statistical tests used for the primary outcome, and a detailed description of the app’s content and protocol.

**Problem:** The Discussion concludes that the app "could replace first-line lifestyle counseling" or "should be offered in primary care." This is an unsupported and potentially dangerous extrapolation. The trial compared the app to a waitlist control, not to standard care or active lifestyle counseling. Furthermore, hypertension guidelines universally recommend multifaceted lifestyle changes (diet, exercise, sodium reduction) as first-line; a single modality cannot be claimed to replace comprehensive counseling based on this data.

**Why it matters:** Making clinical practice recommendations based on a superiority trial against a passive control (waitlist) is misleading. Waitlist controls often yield large effect sizes due to placebo effects and natural history. Claiming replacement of standard care requires head-to-head comparison with that care. This overstatement undermines scientific integrity and poses patient safety risks if clinicians interpret the findings as permission to abandon guideline-directed medical therapy.

**Suggested Action:** Remove the claim that the app can "replace" lifestyle counseling or should be routinely offered in primary care. Rephrase conclusions to suggest the app may *complement* existing lifestyle interventions or serve as an adjunctive tool, explicitly acknowledging that it was not tested against standard care and that further trials are needed.

**Problem:** The reported effect size (9.4 mmHg reduction in SBP) is unusually large for a digital mindfulness intervention compared to a waitlist control, yet no data on confounding variables (e.g., medication changes, diet, exercise) or baseline covariates are provided. Additionally, the manuscript fails to provide confidence intervals (CIs) for this effect size, reporting only p-values.

**Why it matters:** A 9.4 mmHg difference is clinically significant but biologically implausible for mindfulness alone without concurrent lifestyle changes or medication adjustments. The lack of data on concomitant therapies suggests potential confounding, meaning the observed reduction could be due to pharmacological adjustments rather than the intervention. Furthermore, p-values alone do not convey the precision of the estimate; without CIs, clinical significance and robustness cannot be fully assessed.

**Suggested Action:** 1) Report data on medication usage and other lifestyle factors at baseline and follow-up to rule out confounding. 2) Provide 95% confidence intervals for the primary outcome (between-group difference in SBP).

**Problem:** Blood pressure (BP) measurement methodology is insufficiently described. The manuscript states an "automated office device" was used but does not specify: (1) the brand/model and calibration procedures; (2) whether measurements were taken in a standardized quiet environment; (3) the number of readings per visit and how they were averaged; or (4) if ambulatory or home BP monitoring was used. Additionally, it is unclear if participants continued their antihypertensive medications and if dosage changes were recorded.

**Why it matters:** Blood pressure measurement is highly sensitive to technique and device variability. Inconsistent protocols can introduce significant noise or bias. More critically, hypertension management typically involves medication. If medication use was not monitored or controlled, any change in SBP could be due to titration of drugs rather than the mindfulness app, invalidating the causal claim.

**Suggested Action:** Specify the BP device model, calibration status, and measurement protocol (e.g., "average of 3 readings after 5 min rest"). Explicitly state whether antihypertensive medication use was controlled for, and if so, how (e.g., excluded from analysis, adjusted for in models, or reported as a covariate).

**Problem:** The control group was a "waitlist" rather than an active control (e.g., health education app or usual care). In digital health trials, waitlist controls often fail to account for placebo effects, attention bias, and general health engagement. A 9.4 mmHg difference against a passive control may overestimate the specific efficacy of mindfulness.

**Why it matters:** The large effect size may be driven by non-specific factors (e.g., participant engagement with a new app) rather than the mindfulness intervention itself. This limits the generalizability of the results to real-world clinical settings where patients expect active engagement.

**Suggested Action:** Acknowledge this limitation explicitly in the Discussion. Suggest that future trials should use active control groups to isolate the specific effect of mindfulness from general digital health engagement.

**Problem:** Adherence was reported as 71%, but the manuscript does not describe how adherence was defined or measured (e.g., daily login, minutes used). More critically, it is unclear if the analysis was intention-to-treat (ITT) or per-protocol. If ITT, the true effect among adherent users may be larger; if per-protocol, selection bias is introduced. Additionally, no data on attrition/dropout rates, reasons for dropout, or adverse events are provided. There is also no analysis of the relationship between adherence and blood pressure reduction.

**Why it matters:** Understanding adherence patterns and attrition is crucial for interpreting efficacy and safety. High dropout or poor adherence could skew results. Lack of adverse event reporting ignores potential harms (e.g., increased anxiety from mindfulness in some populations). Without an ITT analysis, the benefits of randomization may be lost, potentially overestimating effectiveness.

**Suggested Action:** Define adherence metrics clearly. State whether the primary analysis was ITT or per-protocol. Report participant flow (CONSORT diagram), including numbers randomized, allocated, followed up, and analyzed. Include a table of adverse events. Consider including a sensitivity analysis or correlation between app usage intensity and change in SBP.

**Problem:** The Methods section is extremely brief and lacks standard trial reporting elements beyond those already noted. Specifically, it omits: (1) Sample size calculation and power analysis; (2) Follow-up schedule/timeline.

**Why it matters:** Readers cannot assess whether the study was adequately powered to detect the observed effect or who the results apply to over time. Without a clear timeline, the duration of follow-up and data collection points are ambiguous.

**Suggested Action:** Expand the Methods section to include a sample size justification/power analysis and a detailed follow-up timeline.

**Problem:** Data availability is listed as "on request." Modern scientific standards and many journals require data sharing to enhance transparency and reproducibility. "On request" can be a barrier to verification and secondary analysis.

**Why it matters:** Restrictive data sharing limits the ability of other researchers to verify findings or conduct meta-analyses. Open science standards increasingly require clear, accessible data sharing policies.

**Suggested Action:** Deposit de-identified participant-level data in a recognized repository (e.g., OSF, Dryad) and provide a DOI link in the Declarations section. If this is not possible, specify the conditions under which data will be shared (e.g., "anonymized data available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request").

**Problem:** Citation [2] (Lee K, et al., JAMA. 2021. doi:10.1001/jama.2021.55512) is fabricated. The grounding pack confirms this DOI does not exist in Crossref or OpenAlex.
**Why it matters:** Citing non-existent literature constitutes academic misconduct and invalidates the claim that "prior digital trials concur" with these results, as the supporting evidence is fictional.
**Action:** Remove Citation [2] immediately. Replace with valid, existing references from the literature that support the claim about digital mindfulness interventions, or remove the claim if no such valid evidence exists.

**Problem:** Major protocol deviation regarding the comparator. The registered protocol specifies an **active control (sham relaxation audio)**, but the manuscript reports a **waitlist** control.
**Why it matters:** Switching from an active control to a waitlist significantly changes the trial's internal validity and blinding status. A waitlist control allows for placebo effects and lack of blinding, which can inflate treatment effects compared to an active sham control. This deviation undermines the credibility of the causal inference.
**Action:** Explicitly disclose this deviation in the Methods section. Justify why the protocol was changed post-hoc or pre-registration. Re-analyze or discuss how the lack of active control and blinding might have inflated the observed effect size.

**Problem:** Major protocol deviation regarding the primary outcome timepoint. The registered protocol defines the primary outcome as change in SBP at **24 weeks**, but the manuscript reports and analyzes results at **8 weeks**.
**Why it matters:** Reporting a secondary or interim timepoint as the primary outcome constitutes HARKing (Hypothesizing After Results are Known) if not pre-specified, or simply misrepresents the trial's design. The effect size may differ significantly between 8 and 24 weeks. This deviation invalidates the claim that the results address the registered primary hypothesis.
**Action:** Clearly state in the Methods that the analysis was performed at 8 weeks despite the 24-week registration, or clarify if the protocol was amended. If the 24-week data exists, it should be reported as the primary outcome. If not, the study cannot claim to fulfill its registered primary objective.

**Problem:** Deviation from pre-specified statistical analysis. The protocol mandates an **ANCOVA adjusting for baseline SBP**, but the manuscript presents raw mean differences and p-values without specifying if ANCOVA was used.
**Why it matters:** While the independent recomputation shows the reported t-test/p-value is mathematically consistent with the provided means/SDs, using a simple t-test instead of the pre-specified ANCOVA can lead to biased estimates and reduced power. It is unclear if the reported p < 0.001 comes from the required ANCOVA or an ad-hoc t-test.
**Action:** Explicitly state whether the primary analysis followed the protocol (ANCOVA) or deviated from it. If ANCOVA was used, report the adjusted mean difference and confidence interval. If a t-test was used instead, justify this deviation and acknowledge the potential bias.

**Problem:** Overstated conclusions. The Discussion claims the app "could replace first-line lifestyle counseling" and should be "offered in primary care."
**Why it matters:** Given the critical protocol deviations (wrong comparator, wrong timepoint) and the fabricated citation, the evidence base for such a strong clinical recommendation is nonexistent. Furthermore, an 8-week result from a single-center trial with a waitlist control is insufficient to recommend replacing standard of care.
**Action:** Tone down the conclusions significantly. Acknowledge the limitations imposed by the protocol deviations. Remove the claim about replacing first-line counseling unless supported by robust, valid evidence (which is currently missing due to the fabricated citation and design flaws).

**Protocol Deviation: Primary Outcome Timing and Comparator**

*Problem:* The manuscript reports the primary outcome at 8 weeks using a waitlist control. The verified registered protocol specifies the primary outcome as change in SBP at **24 weeks** and mandates an **active control (sham relaxation audio)**.

*Why it matters:* This constitutes a major deviation from the pre-registered protocol. Using a waitlist instead of an active control introduces significant expectancy bias, potentially inflating the treatment effect. Changing the analysis timepoint post-hoc without justification compromises the trial's internal validity and increases the risk of selective reporting (p-hacking).

*Action:* The authors must explicitly justify these deviations in the Methods section. They should report the 24-week outcomes as the primary result per protocol. If the 8-week data is presented, it must be labeled as exploratory/secondary. The use of a waitlist control must be acknowledged as a limitation that weakens causal inference compared to an active comparator.

**Non-Existent Citation [2]**

*Problem:* Reference [2] (Lee K, et al., JAMA. 2021. doi:10.1001/jama.2021.55512) is cited to support the claim that "digital delivery improves reach" and that "prior digital trials concur." Grounding validation confirms this DOI **does not exist** in Crossref or OpenAlex.

*Why it matters:* Citing a non-existent study to support core conclusions is a serious integrity issue. It suggests fabricated evidence or severe negligence in reference management. The claim that prior trials concur with these specific results is unsupported by the provided bibliography.

*Action:* Remove Reference [2] immediately. Replace it with a valid, existing citation that actually supports the claim about digital delivery reach or concordant trial results. If no such evidence exists, remove the claim that "prior digital trials concur."

**Overstated Conclusions Regarding Clinical Replacement**

*Problem:* The Discussion states the app "could replace first-line lifestyle counseling," and the Abstract concludes it "should be offered in primary care." These claims are disproportionate to the evidence, especially given the protocol deviations (waitlist control) and the lack of long-term (24-week) data as per registration.

*Why it matters:* Suggesting a digital intervention *replace* standard clinical counseling is a high-stakes claim requiring robust, protocol-adherent evidence with active controls. The current study design (post-hoc 8-week analysis vs waitlist) does not support such a definitive clinical recommendation.

*Action:* Tone down the conclusions significantly. Remove the phrase "replace first-line lifestyle counseling." Change the conclusion to reflect that the app *may be an adjunctive tool* pending further validation with active controls and longer-term follow-up as originally planned.

**Missing Justification for Analysis Method Deviation**

*Problem:* The registered protocol pre-specified **ANCOVA adjusting for baseline SBP** as the primary analysis. The manuscript reports simple between-group differences in means (124.6 vs 134.0) and implies a t-test (consistent with the statistical recomputation of p≈2e-7). There is no mention of ANCOVA or adjustment for baseline covariates.

*Why it matters:* While baseline means were identical here, deviating from the pre-specified statistical model without explanation undermines methodological rigor. ANCOVA is generally more powerful and adjusts for any minor imbalances.

*Action:* Clarify why the pre-specified ANCOVA was not used. If ANCOVA was performed, report those adjusted estimates. If a t-test was used instead, provide a strong justification for this deviation from the registered statistical analysis plan.

**Problem:** The Methods section lacks essential details required for replication and validity assessment, including randomization method (e.g., computer-generated sequence), allocation concealment, blinding procedures, and statistical analysis plan. It also fails to define the specific app intervention content or duration of daily use.

**Why it matters:** Without these details, it is impossible to assess selection bias, performance bias, or detection bias. The lack of intervention specificity prevents readers from understanding what constitutes the "mindfulness" exposure.

**Suggested Action:** Expand the Methods section to include: 1) Randomization procedure (sequence generation and allocation concealment); 2) Blinding status of participants, providers, and outcome assessors; 3) Detailed description of the app intervention (content, frequency, duration); 4) Statistical methods used for analysis.

**Problem:** The Discussion claims the app "could replace first-line lifestyle counseling." This conclusion is not supported by the trial design, which compared the app to a waitlist control rather than active lifestyle counseling. Furthermore, no data on other lifestyle factors (diet, exercise) are presented.

**Why it matters:** Replacing established clinical guidelines with unproven digital interventions poses patient safety risks. The claim oversteps the evidence provided by a superiority trial against a passive control.

**Suggested Action:** Remove the statement that the app can replace first-line lifestyle counseling. Instead, frame the findings as complementary to standard care or requiring further head-to-head trials against active lifestyle interventions before such claims can be made.

**Problem:** The Results report a 9.4 mmHg difference but do not provide confidence intervals (CI) for the between-group difference or effect size measures (e.g., Cohen’s d). Only p-values are provided.

**Why it matters:** P-values alone do not convey the precision of the estimate or clinical significance. Confidence intervals are necessary to interpret the magnitude and uncertainty of the treatment effect.

**Suggested Action:** Report the 95% confidence interval for the between-group difference in SBP and provide an effect size metric.

**Problem:** The manuscript reports baseline and 8-week means but does not explicitly state the statistical test used (e.g., ANCOVA, t-test) or how missing data were handled. It also fails to report whether intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis was performed.

**Why it matters:** The choice of statistical test and handling of missing data significantly impacts the validity of the results. Without this information, the robustness of the p < 0.001 finding cannot be verified.

**Suggested Action:** Specify the statistical model used for the primary outcome analysis and describe how missing data were addressed (e.g., multiple imputation, last observation carried forward).

**Problem:** The Methods section does not define the inclusion/exclusion criteria beyond "Adults with stage-1 hypertension." It lacks details on comorbidities, current medications (especially antihypertensives), and baseline health status.

**Why it matters:** Hypertension management is heavily influenced by medication adherence and comorbid conditions. Without knowing if participants were on stable medication regimens, the observed BP reduction could be due to improved medication adherence rather than mindfulness.

**Suggested Action:** Detail the inclusion/exclusion criteria, including requirements for stable antihypertensive medication regimens and exclusion of secondary hypertension causes.

**Problem:** Adherence is reported as 71%, but it is unclear what metric this represents (e.g., days used, minutes engaged, modules completed). 

**Why it matters:** Ambiguous adherence metrics make it difficult to assess the fidelity of the intervention delivery and interpret the dose-response relationship.

**Suggested Action:** Define how adherence was calculated (e.g., percentage of days with at least 10 minutes of app usage).

**Problem:** The study design is described as a randomized controlled trial, but there is no mention of randomization method (e.g., sequence generation, allocation concealment) or blinding. Additionally, the control group is a "waitlist," which introduces significant performance and detection bias, especially for a subjective intervention like mindfulness where participants know they are not receiving the active treatment.
**Why it matters:** Without proper randomization details and blinding (or an active control), the observed 9.4 mmHg difference may be attributed to placebo effects, increased attention from researchers, or regression to the mean rather than the app itself. This severely undermines internal validity.
**Suggested Action:** Detail the randomization sequence generation and allocation concealment methods. Consider using an active control (e.g., health education app) instead of a waitlist to control for non-specific effects. If blinding is impossible, describe steps taken to minimize bias (e.g., blinded outcome assessors).

**Problem:** Blood pressure measurement methodology is insufficiently described. It states "automated office device" but lacks details on protocol (e.g., number of readings, rest period, cuff size, arm position). Furthermore, baseline and 8-week SBP are presented as single means without standard deviations or confidence intervals for the change scores, making it impossible to assess variability or clinical significance beyond the p-value.
**Why it matters:** Hypertension diagnosis and monitoring require strict adherence to measurement protocols (e.g., AHA/ACC guidelines). Inconsistent measurement can introduce significant error. Lack of SDs/CIs prevents readers from evaluating the precision of the estimate and the heterogeneity of response.
**Suggested Action:** Provide a detailed BP measurement protocol compliant with current clinical guidelines. Report mean change in SBP for each group with 95% confidence intervals and standard deviations. Include data on how many readings were averaged per visit.

**Problem:** The manuscript reports 180 participants enrolled but does not provide a CONSORT flow diagram or account for attrition. It is unclear if the n=90 per arm in the results represents intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis or complete-case analysis. If dropouts occurred, their exclusion could bias results.
**Why it matters:** Attrition bias is a common threat to RCT validity. Without knowing how many participants dropped out and why, or if ITT was used, the generalizability and robustness of the findings are questionable.
**Suggested Action:** Include a CONSORT flow diagram showing numbers randomized, allocated, followed up, and analyzed. Explicitly state whether analysis was ITT or per-protocol. If per-protocol, justify why and discuss potential bias.

**Problem:** The discussion claims the app "could replace first-line lifestyle counseling," which is an overstatement not supported by the data. The trial only compared the app to a waitlist, not to standard care or formal lifestyle counseling. Additionally, no safety monitoring or adverse events are reported.
**Why it matters:** Making strong clinical recommendations (replacing standard care) without head-to-head comparison against that standard care is scientifically unjustified and potentially harmful if implemented prematurely. Lack of safety data ignores potential risks (e.g., anxiety exacerbation from mindfulness).
**Suggested Action:** Tone down the conclusion to reflect that the app *may be an adjunct* to, not a replacement for, standard care. Acknowledge the lack of comparison to active lifestyle interventions. Add a section on adverse events or safety monitoring.

**Problem:** The Methods section is extremely sparse, lacking essential details required for replication and appraisal. Missing elements include: eligibility criteria (inclusion/exclusion), sample size calculation/power analysis, statistical methods used to derive the p-value, description of the app content/duration, and how adherence was defined and handled in analysis.
**Why it matters:** Without these details, the study cannot be replicated, and readers cannot assess if the sample size was adequate to detect the effect or if the statistical tests were appropriate.
**Suggested Action:** Expand Methods to include: (1) Detailed eligibility criteria; (2) Sample size calculation with assumptions; (3) Description of the app intervention (content, duration, frequency); (4) Statistical analysis plan (e.g., ANCOVA, mixed models); (5) Definition of adherence and how it was incorporated into the analysis (e.g., as a covariate or in sensitivity analyses).

**Problem:** Adherence is reported as 71%, but it is unclear how this was calculated (e.g., days used/total days) and whether adherence differed between groups. There is no analysis of the relationship between adherence and BP reduction.
**Why it matters:** Understanding the dose-response relationship is critical for digital health interventions. If only highly adherent users benefit, the real-world effectiveness may be lower.
**Suggested Action:** Report adherence metrics for both groups separately. Conduct a sensitivity analysis or correlation analysis to examine if higher adherence correlates with greater BP reduction.

**Problem:** The conclusion that the app "could replace first-line lifestyle counseling" is unsupported by the study design. The control group was a waitlist, not an active comparator receiving standard lifestyle counseling (e.g., DASH diet, exercise). 

**Why it matters:** This is an overgeneralization and potentially misleading claim. A superiority trial against a waitlist does not demonstrate non-inferiority or equivalence to established interventions. Major clinical guidelines recommend multimodal lifestyle changes as first-line; mindfulness is typically adjunctive. Making such strong claims without direct comparative evidence risks misleading clinicians.

**Suggested Action:** Revise the conclusion to reflect that the app is superior to no intervention/waitlist, rather than claiming it can replace existing standard-of-care counseling. Suggest future head-to-head trials against lifestyle counseling and frame the app as a potential adjunctive tool.

**Problem:** The study lacks blinding of outcome assessors and uses a waitlist control, introducing significant performance bias, detection/observer bias, and regression to the mean. Participants knew their group assignment, and it is unclear if BP operators were blinded. Furthermore, there is no active control (e.g., sham app or health education) to account for placebo effects or attention from researchers.

**Why it matters:** Blood pressure is highly susceptible to expectancy effects and white-coat variability. Without blinding or an active control, the observed 9.4 mmHg difference may reflect natural fluctuation, regression to the mean, or placebo effects rather than physiological change induced by the mindfulness content. This severely threatens internal validity.

**Suggested Action:** Explicitly state whether outcome assessors were blinded to group allocation. If not, acknowledge this as a major limitation. Discuss the limitations of using a waitlist control versus an active control in the Limitations section and soften causal language regarding efficacy.

**Problem:** The analysis assumes Intent-to-Treat (ITT) or Complete-Case analysis without reporting how missing data were handled. With only 180 participants, attrition can severely bias results. The manuscript does not report the number of participants who completed the trial vs. those lost to follow-up, nor does it specify the statistical method for handling missing outcomes.

**Why it matters:** If dropouts are non-random (e.g., those with higher BP or lower adherence left the app group), the reported mean difference is biased. Standard ITT requires imputation for missing outcomes; simple deletion (complete-case) violates randomization assumptions if data are not Missing Completely at Random (MCAR).

**Suggested Action:** Report the flow of participants through each stage of the trial (CONSORT diagram). Specify the statistical method used to handle missing data (e.g., multiple imputation, mixed models with maximum likelihood). If complete-case analysis was used, justify why MCAR is plausible or perform sensitivity analyses.

**Problem:** The measurement protocol for blood pressure is insufficiently described. It states SBP was measured by an "automated office device" but does not specify the number of readings, rest period, cuff size appropriateness, patient position, or whether measurements were taken at the same time of day. Additionally, the make/model of the device and calibration procedures are omitted.

**Why it matters:** Blood pressure is variable. Inadequate measurement protocols (e.g., single reading, no rest) increase within-subject variance, reduce power, and introduce measurement error bias. For a trial claiming high precision, standardized BP measurement guidelines (e.g., AHA/ACC) must be followed and reported to ensure reproducibility.

**Suggested Action:** Detail the BP measurement protocol: number of readings averaged, duration of quiet rest prior to measurement, patient position, device calibration status, and how the final SBP value was calculated.

**Problem:** The results report only a p-value (p < 0.001) and point estimate (9.4 mmHg) without a Confidence Interval (CI). The standard deviation is provided, but the standard error of the difference or CI is missing. It is also unclear if the 9.4 mmHg is an unadjusted difference in final values or an adjusted treatment effect.

**Why it matters:** P-values do not convey the precision of the estimate. A wide CI might indicate that the true effect could be clinically trivial or excessively large. Reporting CIs allows readers to assess clinical significance and statistical uncertainty, which is essential for interpreting a 9.4 mmHg reduction in hypertension management.

**Suggested Action:** Report the 95% Confidence Interval for the between-group difference in SBP change. Clarify whether the reported difference is unadjusted or adjusted (e.g., via ANCOVA) and describe the model if adjusted.

**Problem:** Eligibility criteria are vague: "Adults with stage-1 hypertension." It is unclear if this was defined by untreated BP, treated BP, or specific thresholds. There is no mention of exclusion criteria (e.g., secondary hypertension, pregnancy, severe comorbidities) or whether participants were on stable antihypertensive medication regimens.

**Why it matters:** Heterogeneity in baseline hypertension severity, etiology, and medication use affects response to interventions. Without clear inclusion/exclusion criteria and control for medication changes, the sample may not be representative, and medication changes during the trial act as a major unaccounted confounder.

**Suggested Action:** Define stage-1 hypertension numerically. Specify if participants were treatment-naïve or on stable medications. List key exclusion criteria. Include data on changes in antihypertensive medication usage between groups during the trial.

**Problem:** Adherence is reported as 71%, but it is unclear how this was defined (e.g., daily usage, minutes per day) and whether adherence was included in the analysis (as-modified vs. intent-to-treat). There is no report of baseline characteristics to confirm balance between groups, nor an analysis of the relationship between dose/response and SBP change.

**Why it matters:** Without baseline tables, we cannot verify that randomization achieved balance in key prognostic factors. Reporting only adherence without linking it to outcome limits understanding of the mechanism. If analysis was per-protocol, it introduces selection bias; if ITT, high non-adherence may bias results towards the null.

**Suggested Action:** Include a table of baseline characteristics for both groups. Clarify if the primary analysis was ITT or per-protocol. Define adherence metrics clearly and consider reporting an adjusted analysis or sensitivity analysis accounting for adherence.

**Problem:** The manuscript states "Funding: none" and "Competing interests: none," yet it promotes a specific smartphone app. There is no disclosure of the app's developer, ownership, or whether the authors have any financial stake (equity, patents) in the technology.

**Why it matters:** Even if direct funding was not received for the trial, intellectual property rights or equity stakes in the app company constitute competing interests that must be disclosed to assess potential bias. Transparency regarding the source of the intervention is essential.

**Suggested Action:** Disclose the name of the app and its developer. Explicitly state any relationships (employment, equity, patents) between authors and the app's development entity.

**Problem:** Data availability is listed as "on request." There is no mention of trial registration (e.g., ClinicalTrials.gov) or a pre-specified analysis plan.

**Why it matters:** Lack of trial registration increases the risk of selective reporting and p-hacking. Transparency in data sharing is increasingly required by journals and ethical standards.

**Suggested Action:** Provide the trial registration number and date of registration relative to participant enrollment. Deposit anonymized data in a public repository if possible.

**Problem:** The Abstract states "SBP fell by 9.4 mmHg more in the app group than control" but lacks context on baseline values. While the Results section clarifies baseline SBP was identical (134 mmHg), the Abstract does not include this.

**Why it matters:** Readers cannot assess the magnitude of change relative to starting points without baseline data in the abstract.

**Suggested Action:** Include baseline mean SBP for both groups in the Abstract Results section for completeness.

**Problem:** The discussion cites "Prior digital trials concur [2]" but does not discuss heterogeneity or limitations of prior evidence.

**Why it matters:** Simply citing concurrence without critical appraisal weakens the scholarly contribution. It fails to position this study within the broader context (e.g., did other apps fail? What was different here?).

**Suggested Action:** Briefly compare this result with the cited literature, noting similarities in effect size or differences in methodology that might explain consistency.

**Problem:** The manuscript fails to report a sample size calculation or power analysis. With n=180 and an SD of 12 mmHg, the study is powered for specific effect sizes, but without reporting this, readers cannot assess if the sample was adequate for the primary hypothesis or if the result is underpowered/overpowered.

**Why it matters:** Without a priori power analysis, it is impossible to determine if the null hypothesis rejection (p < 0.001) is robust or if the study suffered from type II error risks for smaller but clinically relevant effects. It also raises questions about resource allocation and ethical recruitment.

**Suggested Action:** Include a section in Methods detailing the sample size calculation, specifying the assumed effect size, alpha level (typically 0.05), power (typically 80-90%), and variance estimates used for planning.

**Problem:** Critical omission of randomization methodology, allocation concealment, and blinding details. The text states participants were "randomized 1:1" but provides no information on the randomization sequence generation, allocation concealment, or whether assessors/outcomes were blinded.

**Why it matters:** Without these details, the risk of selection bias and performance/detection bias cannot be assessed. In a trial with a large effect size (9.4 mmHg), lack of blinding is a major threat to internal validity, as participants and clinicians may unconsciously influence blood pressure measurements or reporting.

**Suggested Action:** Explicitly describe the randomization method (e.g., computer-generated sequence), allocation concealment mechanism (e.g., sealed envelopes or central web-based system), and blinding status of outcome assessors. If unblinded, acknowledge this as a significant limitation.

**Problem:** The control group is a "waitlist" with no active comparator or standard-of-care monitoring. There is no mention of blinding for outcome assessors or participants, and the waitlist provides no active comparison for the specific intervention (mindfulness) and fails to control for placebo effects or regression to the mean.

**Why it matters:** In digital health trials, expectant care (waitlist) is a weak comparator. Participants in the app group may improve due to simply receiving attention or monitoring their BP more closely (Hawthorne effect), rather than the mindfulness content itself. Without an active control (e.g., a sham app or standard care education), internal validity is severely compromised.

**Suggested Action:** Replace the waitlist with an active comparator arm (e.g., a health education app without mindfulness components) to isolate the specific effect of mindfulness. If this is not possible, acknowledge the lack of blinding and active control as a major limitation and discuss potential placebo effects.

**Problem:** Inadequate description of the intervention and control condition. The manuscript mentions "the app" but does not specify its name, content structure, duration of daily sessions, or active components. Similarly, the "waitlist" control is described only as a waitlist, with no mention of usual care or monitoring frequency.

**Why it matters:** Reproducibility requires precise intervention details. Furthermore, without knowing if the control group received any attention or standard care, it is unclear if the effect is due to mindfulness specifically or simply increased engagement/attention (Hawthorne effect). The claim that the app "replaces first-line lifestyle counseling" cannot be supported if the control did not receive such counseling.

**Suggested Action:** Provide a detailed description of the app’s content, user interface, and required daily time commitment. Clarify what the waitlist group received during the 8-week period (e.g., standard care only? No contact?). Include an intervention fidelity assessment if applicable.

**Problem:** Blood pressure measurement protocol lacks critical validity safeguards. The text states SBP was measured by an "automated office device" but omits details on calibration, number of readings averaged, rest period prior to measurement, and whether measurements were blinded to group allocation.

**Why it matters:** Office BP is highly susceptible to observer bias and white-coat effects. If the measurer knew the participant's group assignment, results are biased. Furthermore, without specifying averaging protocols (e.g., 2-3 readings after 5 mins rest), the precision of the outcome is questionable, threatening construct validity.

**Suggested Action:** Specify if measurements were taken by blinded personnel, report the number of readings averaged per visit, and describe the pre-measurement rest protocol. Ideally, consider ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) for higher validity.

**Problem:** The manuscript does not include a CONSORT flow diagram or detailed attrition data. It states n=180 randomized but does not report how many participants completed the 8-week period, how many were excluded from analysis, or if an intention-to-treat (ITT) approach was used.

**Why it matters:** High dropout rates or differential attrition between arms can severely bias results, especially in behavioral interventions. Without knowing the final analyzed sample size and handling of missing data, the validity of the primary outcome is unclear.

**Suggested Action:** Include a CONSORT flow diagram detailing recruitment, randomization, follow-up, and analysis numbers. Explicitly state whether an intention-to-treat or per-protocol analysis was conducted and how missing data were handled.

**Problem:** Lack of handling for missing data and attrition. The manuscript states n=180 at baseline but does not report how many participants completed the 8-week follow-up or were included in the final analysis (intention-to-treat vs. per-protocol). The analysis appears to use a simple comparison of means without accounting for baseline values or using an ITT framework with appropriate handling of missing data.

**Why it matters:** Comparing post-intervention means without adjusting for baseline can reduce power and introduce bias if randomization imbalance occurred. More critically, excluding participants with missing data (complete-case analysis) biases results if dropouts are related to treatment response. With 71% adherence, attrition is likely non-random.

**Suggested Action:** Report the number of participants analyzed in each arm. State whether an ITT analysis was performed. If missing data exist, describe the method used (e.g., multiple imputation, mixed-effects models) rather than simple exclusion.

**Problem:** The primary outcome is reported only as a p-value (p < 0.001) without a confidence interval (CI). While the point estimate (9.4 mmHg) and SD are given, the precision of this estimate is not explicitly quantified for the reader. Additionally, no standardized effect size (e.g., Cohen’s d) is provided.

**Why it matters:** P-values alone do not convey the clinical uncertainty or the range of plausible effects. A CI allows clinicians to assess if the lower bound remains clinically significant. Effect sizes are necessary to contextualize the magnitude of change relative to variability.

**Suggested Action:** Report the 95% confidence interval for the between-group difference in SBP (e.g., "9.4 mmHg [95% CI: X.X to Y.Y]"). Include a standardized effect size measure to facilitate comparison with other hypertension interventions.

**Problem:** Adherence is reported as a single aggregate percentage (71%) without distribution or association with outcomes. There is no analysis of whether higher adherence correlated with greater BP reduction.

**Why it matters:** If the effect is driven only by high adherers, the average treatment effect may overestimate the benefit for the general population who may adhere less. Understanding the dose-response relationship is critical for clinical implementation. In digital health trials, 'efficacy' (under ideal conditions) differs from 'effectiveness' (real-world use).

**Suggested Action:** Provide a breakdown of adherence (e.g., median days used). Perform and report an analysis examining the correlation between adherence levels and SBP change. Discuss whether the results represent efficacy or effectiveness.

**Problem:** The conclusion that the app "could replace first-line lifestyle counseling" is an overstatement not supported by the data. The study did not compare the app to standard lifestyle counseling, and replacing established clinical guidelines with a single digital intervention based on one RCT is clinically unsafe and unwarranted.

**Why it matters:** This represents a logical leap beyond the evidence. Without a head-to-head comparison against current standard of care, claims of replacement are unsubstantiated and potentially harmful if implemented prematurely. It misrepresents the trial's capability to compare against standard of care.

**Suggested Action:** Temper the conclusion to state that the app "may serve as an adjunctive tool" or "shows promise comparable to prior interventions," avoiding definitive claims about replacing existing clinical guidelines without direct comparative evidence.

**Problem:** Eligibility criteria and baseline comparability are insufficiently described. The text mentions "Adults with stage-1 hypertension" but does not define the specific SBP/DBP range, exclusion criteria (e.g., secondary hypertension, current medication changes), or confirm randomization balance for key covariates (age, sex, BMI, baseline meds).

**Why it matters:** Without strict eligibility and confirmation of baseline equivalence, confounding variables may explain the outcome. For instance, if one group had more patients already adjusting medications, the BP drop might not be due to the app.

**Suggested Action:** Add a detailed table of baseline characteristics (demographics, clinical factors, medication use) for both arms. Define precise inclusion/exclusion criteria, particularly regarding antihypertensive medication stability.

**Problem:** Background literature citations are embedded in the Methods section. Sentences such as "Mindfulness improves autonomic balance... [1]" and "digital delivery improves reach [2]" belong in the Introduction or Discussion, not Methods.

**Why it matters:** The Methods section should strictly describe what was done in the study. Including background justification here disrupts the logical flow and violates standard scientific writing conventions (IMRaD structure).

**Suggested Action:** Move all background context, rationale, and references to prior work into the Introduction or Discussion sections. Keep the Methods section focused solely on participant recruitment, randomization, intervention delivery, outcome measurement, and statistical analysis.

**Problem:** The discussion cites "Prior digital trials concur [2]" but does not critically appraise the consistency of this evidence. Reference [2] is a single citation; broader context regarding conflicting or null findings in digital mindfulness for hypertension is missing.

**Why it matters:** Over-reliance on supportive citations while ignoring contradictory evidence creates confirmation bias and overstates the certainty of the findings.

**Suggested Action:** Broaden the discussion to include a balanced review of existing literature, acknowledging any trials that found no significant effect of mindfulness apps on BP. This strengthens the credibility of the authors' interpretation.

**Problem:** Data availability is stated as "on request" without a repository or DOI. This violates modern transparency standards (e.g., TRIPOD, CONSORT) and hinders reproducibility and meta-analysis.

**Why it matters:** Vague statements hinder reproducibility and verification of results.

**Suggested Action:** Deposit de-identified data in a public repository (e.g., OSF, Dryad) and provide the accession number/DOI in the manuscript. If restricted due to privacy, cite the specific ethical/legal constraints.

**Problem:** Ambiguous reporting of standard deviation. The text states "Group SD was 12 mmHg" without specifying if this is the pooled SD, the SD for each group individually, or the SD of the difference.

**Why it matters:** Precision in statistical reporting is essential for readers to interpret variability and perform meta-analyses.

**Suggested Action:** Specify whether the 12 mmHg SD applies to both groups equally, is a pooled estimate, or refers to a specific group. Report separate SDs for each arm if they differ.

**Problem:** The manuscript lacks essential methodological details required to assess internal validity, reproducibility, and adherence to CONSORT guidelines. Specifically, it omits: 1) Participant eligibility criteria (inclusion/exclusion); 2) Randomization procedures (sequence generation, block size, stratification, allocation concealment); 3) Blinding status (specifically for outcome assessors, despite being open-label for participants); 4) A precise operational definition of the comparator ('usual care'); and 5) The statistical analysis plan (handling of missing data, intention-to-treat vs. per-protocol approaches).

**Why it matters:** Without these details, readers cannot determine if selection bias, performance bias, or detection bias influenced the results. The claim of a 'randomized trial' cannot be verified, and the lack of statistical method description prevents verification of reported p-values and risk ratios.

**Suggested Action:** Rewrite the Methods section to adhere to CONSORT guidelines. Include explicit subsections for Participants (with eligibility criteria), Randomization (sequence generation and allocation concealment), Intervention (detailed description of coaching protocol and control arm), Outcomes, Sample Size Calculation, and Statistical Analysis.

**Problem:** The results section is severely under-reported. It provides only aggregate percentages for the primary outcome and mean weight loss, lacking: 1) Confidence intervals (CI) for the risk ratio and weight difference; 2) Standard deviations or interquartile ranges for continuous variables; 3) Data on attrition/dropout rates and reasons for withdrawal; 4) Adverse events or safety data (critical for dietary interventions in diabetes, e.g., hypoglycemia, lipid changes); 5) Baseline comparability of groups; and 6) Data on adherence to the coaching program and changes in diabetes medications.

**Why it matters:** P-values alone are insufficient for interpreting clinical significance. The absence of CIs prevents assessment of precision. Missing attrition data raises concerns about attrition bias. Lack of safety data is a major ethical and clinical oversight for a diabetes intervention, and omitting medication changes confounds the interpretation of HbA1c improvements.

**Suggested Action:** Expand the Results section to include: 1) A baseline table comparing groups; 2) Primary outcome with 95% CI for the risk ratio; 3) Secondary outcomes (weight, etc.) with mean differences and CIs/SDs; 4) A CONSORT flow diagram detailing recruitment, allocation, follow-up, and analysis numbers; 5) A dedicated subsection on adverse events and safety laboratory values; and 6) Data on adherence metrics and medication changes.

**Problem:** The conclusion states that low-carb coaching 'should become standard care' based on a single, short-term (6-month), open-label trial with no reported long-term sustainability metrics or comprehensive safety data. The discussion is brief and fails to address limitations (e.g., open-label design, potential biases), alternative explanations, or generalizability. It also lacks context from the broader literature beyond two citations.

**Why it matters:** This overstates the evidence. A 6-month trial is insufficient to establish 'standard of care,' which requires evidence of long-term efficacy, safety, and cost-effectiveness. The jump from a statistically significant p-value to a policy recommendation is not supported by the limited data presented and may mislead clinical practice.

**Suggested Action:** Tone down the conclusion to reflect the study's scope and limitations. State that low-carb coaching 'may improve short-term glycemic control' and call for larger, longer-duration trials with safety monitoring before recommending it as standard care. Expand the Discussion to interpret results in light of the open-label nature, compare with other relevant trials, and explicitly discuss limitations.

**Problem:** The data availability statement is 'on request,' which is increasingly considered insufficient for transparency and reproducibility in modern research. Additionally, the funding source is listed as 'none,' which raises questions given the scale of a 240-person RCT involving telehealth coaching and laboratory testing.

**Why it matters:** 'On request' data availability hinders independent verification of results and meta-analysis. Lack of clear funding disclosure may raise concerns about potential conflicts of interest or sustainability of the intervention model.

**Suggested Action:** Deposit de-identified individual participant data in a recognized public repository (e.g., Dryad, Zenodo, Figshare) and provide the DOI/link in the manuscript. Clarify how the trial was funded if no external grant was involved (e.g., institutional support).

**Problem:** The manuscript lacks a clear distinction between primary and secondary outcomes. Weight loss is presented as a key result but was not defined as a co-primary outcome in the Methods or Abstract. The abstract itself is incomplete, lacking background, detailed methods (sample size calculation, setting), and balanced conclusions.

**Why it matters:** Multiple unadjusted comparisons increase the risk of Type I error. Readers may misinterpret weight loss as a prespecified primary endpoint rather than an exploratory finding. An incomplete abstract reduces its utility for initial screening.

**Suggested Action:** Clearly define primary and secondary outcomes in the Abstract and Methods. If weight was secondary, report its p-value with caution or label it as exploratory. Restructure the abstract to include Background, Methods (with sample size justification), Results (including confidence intervals), and Conclusion sections.

**Problem:** The abstract is incomplete. It lacks a background section, detailed methods (sample size calculation, setting), and a balanced conclusion.

**Why it matters:** Abstracts should stand alone as a complete summary of the paper. Readers often rely on abstracts for initial screening; missing key methodological details reduces its utility.

**Suggested Action:** Restructure the abstract to include Background, Methods (with sample size justification and setting), Results (including confidence intervals), and Conclusion sections.

**Problem:** The Methods section lacks essential details required to assess internal validity, reproducibility, and generalizability. Specifically, it omits: (1) precise inclusion/exclusion criteria for participants; (2) a detailed description of the 'usual care' control group intervention; (3) the specific content, frequency, duration, and mode of the 'low-carb coaching' intervention; (4) the randomization sequence generation and allocation concealment methods; and (5) how adherence to the dietary protocol was measured or verified.

**Why it matters:** Without these details, readers cannot determine if selection bias, performance bias, or attention bias compromised the results. The claim of a 'randomized trial' cannot be fully verified, and the effect size cannot be contextualized against specific patient characteristics. Furthermore, without adherence data, it is impossible to distinguish whether observed effects are due to the dietary composition itself or simply increased engagement/coaching.

**Suggested Action:** Expand the Methods section to include: (1) precise inclusion/exclusion criteria; (2) a detailed description of the control arm's intervention; (3) randomization sequence generation and allocation concealment methods; (4) a comprehensive protocol for the coaching intervention (including materials, frequency, and dietary targets); and (5) methods used to assess participant adherence (e.g., food diaries, biomarkers).

**Problem:** The Results section reports primarily point estimates and binary outcomes (proportion achieving HbA1c < 7.0%) without providing measures of uncertainty or baseline comparability data. Specifically, it lacks: (1) a table of baseline demographics and clinical characteristics to confirm group balance; (2) continuous outcome data (mean change in HbA1c) with confidence intervals; (3) measures of variability (standard deviations or standard errors) for all reported metrics, including weight loss; and (4) critical safety/adverse event data (e.g., hypoglycemia, lipid changes, medication adjustments).

**Why it matters:** Baseline imbalances could confound the observed differences. Without standard deviations or confidence intervals, the precision of the estimates is unknown, making it impossible to assess clinical significance or perform meta-analyses. The absence of safety data is a major concern for dietary interventions in diabetic populations, as low-carb diets can impact lipid profiles and hypoglycemia risk.

**Suggested Action:** Add a Table 1 presenting baseline demographics and clinical characteristics by arm. Report mean changes in HbA1c and weight with 95% CIs and standard deviations for both arms. Include a comprehensive report of adverse events, particularly hypoglycemic episodes, lipid panel changes, and any adjustments to diabetes medications.

**Problem:** The conclusion that 'Low-carb coaching should become standard care' is overstated and not supported by the limited evidence provided. The trial is open-label (high risk of bias), short-term (6 months), lacks blinding, and does not report long-term sustainability or comprehensive safety data.

**Why it matters:** Overstating conclusions can lead to premature clinical implementation of an intervention that may have unreported risks or limited durability. Standard care recommendations require robust evidence from multiple rigorous trials, often including longer follow-up and blinded assessments where possible. The open-label design introduces significant performance and detection bias, weakening the strength of this recommendation.

**Suggested Action:** Tone down the conclusion to reflect the study's limitations. Suggest phrasing such as: 'These findings suggest low-carbohydrate coaching may improve short-term glycemic control, but larger, longer-term, and more rigorously controlled trials are needed before recommending it as standard care.' Explicitly discuss the open-label design, lack of long-term data, and potential safety concerns as limitations affecting the strength of the evidence.

**Problem:** The manuscript states the study is 'open-label' but fails to describe how blinding was handled for outcome assessment or how bias from the lack of blinding was mitigated. There is no discussion of the potential impact of performance bias (participant behavior) or detection bias (assessor judgment), particularly for subjective outcomes like self-reported weight or adherence.

**Why it matters:** In open-label trials, participants and providers know the assignment, which can influence behavior and reporting. While HbA1c is an objective measure, other outcomes may be biased. The impact of the open-label design on the primary outcome needs clarification to properly interpret the internal validity.

**Suggested Action:** Clarify if outcome assessors were blinded to group assignment. If not, discuss the potential for performance bias and detection bias in the Limitations section more thoroughly.

**Problem:** The Abstract is severely truncated and lacks key components required by CONSORT guidelines for randomized trials. It omits: (1) setting and participant details; (2) specific interventions compared; (3) baseline values or range of HbA1c; (4) absolute risk reduction, number needed to treat, or confidence intervals for the primary outcome; and (5) a balanced statement on limitations. Additionally, it ambiguously reports weight loss without specifying which arm achieved the result.

**Why it matters:** The Abstract is often the only part read by clinicians and policymakers. An incomplete abstract fails to provide sufficient context for interpreting the results and may mislead readers about the strength of the evidence or the specific group benefiting from the intervention.

**Suggested Action:** Rewrite the Abstract to include: (1) Study setting and participant numbers with brief eligibility criteria; (2) Clear description of both interventions; (3) Key numerical results including confidence intervals for the primary outcome and explicit attribution of secondary outcomes (e.g., weight loss) to specific arms; (4) A sentence on limitations (e.g., open-label, short duration); and (5) A cautious conclusion aligned with the main text.

**Problem:** The 'Data availability' statement is vague ('on request').

**Why it matters:** Modern publication standards increasingly require transparent data sharing to facilitate reproducibility and secondary analysis. Vague statements hinder scientific integrity and often lead to non-disclosure or delayed access.

**Suggested Action:** Specify the repository where data will be deposited (e.g., Dryad, Zenodo) or provide a clear protocol for how data requests will be handled, including any restrictions due to patient privacy.

**Outcome Switching (Primary vs. Secondary Outcome):** The manuscript identifies the proportion achieving HbA1c < 7.0% as the primary outcome. However, the registered protocol explicitly defines **mean change in HbA1c (%)** as the primary outcome, with the proportion reaching target as a pre-specified secondary outcome. Reporting a secondary outcome as primary without justification constitutes outcome switching, which introduces selection bias and inflates the risk of Type I error.

*Why it matters:* This undermines the validity of the main conclusion and the statistical power calculations likely performed for the original primary outcome.

*Suggested Action:* Re-analyze the data using the registered primary outcome (mean change in HbA1c). If the authors insist on highlighting the proportion, they must clearly label it as a secondary or exploratory finding and adjust for multiple comparisons if not pre-specified as such.

**Retraction of Key Reference:** Reference [2] (Hallberg SJ, et al., Diabetes Ther. 2018) is cited to support the extension of prior dietary trials. Grounding verification confirms this article was **retracted in 2022** due to data concerns.

*Why it matters:* Citing retracted literature compromises the integrity of the evidence base and suggests a lack of rigorous reference validation.

*Suggested Action:* Remove Reference [2] immediately. Replace with valid, non-retracted studies that support the discussion claims, or remove the claim if no other valid evidence exists.

**Statistical Error in P-value:** The manuscript reports a p-value of 0.004 for the comparison of proportions (52% vs 38%, n=120/arm). Independent recomputation confirms the Risk Ratio (1.37) is correct, but the two-proportion z-test yields **p ≈ 0.03**, not 0.004.

*Why it matters:* The reported p-value significantly overstates the statistical significance of the result. While still statistically significant at α=0.05, the exaggeration misleads readers regarding the strength of the evidence.

*Suggested Action:* Correct the p-value to approximately 0.03 in the Abstract and Results sections. Ensure all other statistical tests in the manuscript are re-verified for accuracy.

**Missing Analysis Methodology:** The registered protocol mandates **intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis with multiple imputation** for missing HbA1c data. The manuscript Methods section states 'Outcomes assessed at 6 months' but fails to specify the handling of missing data or confirm adherence to the ITT plan.

*Why it matters:* Without specifying how missing data were handled, it is impossible to verify if the analysis adhered to the pre-specified protocol. Deviations from multiple imputation (e.g., complete-case analysis) can introduce bias and reduce power.
